title: The role of endocannabinoids in natural stress recovery
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
Your body has a built-in recovery system. You probably didn't know about it.

Here's something I wish someone had told me years ago. Your body makes its own cannabis-like molecules. They're called endocannabinoids, and they're one of the most powerful tools you have for recovering from stress and trauma. I'm not talking about getting high. I'm talking about your nervous system's natural ability to calm itself down, reset, and come back to baseline after something hard happens.

I spent years thinking I was broken because I couldn't just "bounce back" from stress. My nervous system would get activated, and it would stay activated. For days. Sometimes weeks. I tried meditation, breathing exercises, all the things. And they helped, a little. But I was missing the bigger picture. My endocannabinoid system was struggling to do its job, and I didn't even know it existed.

Stay with me here. This matters more than you think.

What are endocannabinoids and why should you care?

In the 1990s, a researcher named Raphael Mechoulam identified the endocannabinoid system. He's the scientist who first discovered THC in cannabis, but his real contribution was finding that your body makes its own version of these compounds. Endocannabinoids are signaling molecules that help regulate nearly every system in your body. Sleep, appetite, pain, inflammation, mood, and yes, stress recovery.

Here's the thing. When you experience stress, your body releases cortisol and adrenaline. That's the fight-or-flight response. It's designed to help you survive a threat, then shut off once the threat is gone. But for many of us with trauma histories, that shutdown doesn't happen properly. The stress response stays on. And that's where endocannabinoids come in.

Endocannabinoids act like a brake on your stress response. They tell your nervous system, "Okay, we're safe now. You can calm down." They reduce the release of stress hormones. They dampen the activation of your amygdala, that part of your brain that screams "DANGER" at everything. They help your body return to what Stephen Porges calls the ventral vagal state, that place of safety and connection.

Think about it. Your body literally has a built-in system for coming back to calm. It's not something you have to learn. It's not a skill you acquire. It's biology. But here's the problem. Chronic stress, trauma, and modern life can damage how well this system works. Your endocannabinoid receptors can become less sensitive. Your body might stop producing enough endocannabinoids. The brake stops working as well as it should.

Wild, right? The very system designed to help you recover can get worn out by too much stress.

How trauma changes your endocannabinoid system

Bessel van der Kolk wrote extensively about how trauma reshapes the brain and body. And his work, along with others, points to something crucial. Trauma doesn't just live in your memories. It lives in your nervous system. And it directly affects your endocannabinoid signaling.

Studies have shown that people with PTSD often have lower levels of endocannabinoids like anandamide, sometimes called the "bliss molecule." Their receptors are also different. They're either overactive or underactive, depending on the person and the type of trauma. The system gets dysregulated. It can't do its job of putting the brakes on stress.

This explains so much of what I experienced. That feeling of being on edge all the time. The way my body would react to small triggers as if they were life-threatening. The insomnia, the chronic tension, the digestive problems. All of it connected to a nervous system that couldn't reset.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It walks through how trauma gets stored in the body and what you can do about it. Van der Kolk doesn't specifically focus on endocannabinoids, but the framework he provides helps you understand why your body reacts the way it does.

Here's what I want you to know. Your endocannabinoid system isn't broken forever. It's just struggling. And there are things you can do to support it.

Practical ways to support your endocannabinoid system naturally

You don't need to smoke cannabis to support your endocannabinoid system. In fact, for many trauma survivors, cannabis can make things worse. It can increase anxiety, trigger dissociation, or just make you feel more disconnected from your body. What you need are gentle, consistent practices that help your body produce and use its own endocannabinoids more effectively.

Let me share what's actually helped me and what the research supports.

Movement that feels good, not punishing. Your endocannabinoid system responds to moderate exercise. But here's the catch. Intense, forced exercise can actually increase cortisol. What works better is movement that you enjoy. Walking, stretching, gentle yoga, dancing in your kitchen. The goal isn't to burn calories or build muscle. The goal is to signal to your nervous system that you're safe and present in your body. When you move in a way that feels good, your body releases endocannabinoids. It's called the runner's high, but you don't have to run to get it.

Cold exposure, done carefully. Brief cold exposure, like a cold shower or splashing cold water on your face, can boost endocannabinoid levels. But please, if you have a trauma history, go slow. Cold can be triggering. Start with just 30 seconds of cool water at the end of your shower. See how your body responds. Notice what happens. Don't force it.

Social connection with safe people. This one is huge. Your endocannabinoid system is closely tied to your social engagement system, what Stephen Porges calls the ventral vagal pathway. When you're with people who feel safe, your body releases oxytocin and endocannabinoids. It's a feedback loop. Safety helps your endocannabinoid system work better, and a healthy endocannabinoid system helps you feel safer. But if you don't have safe people in your life, that's okay. Start with pets, nature, or even a therapist you trust.

Mindfulness that's actually mindful. Not the kind where you force yourself to sit still and breathe while your nervous system is screaming. That can retraumatize you. Instead, try short moments of noticing. Notice the feeling of your feet on the floor. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Notice a sound in the room. Just a few seconds. That's enough. These brief moments of present-moment awareness have been shown to increase endocannabinoid levels over time.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down polyvagal theory into exercises you can actually do. It's not theory. It's practice.

Sleep, but not the way you think. I know everyone says sleep is important. But for trauma survivors, sleep is complicated. Hypervigilance makes it hard to fall asleep. Nightmares make it hard to stay asleep. Your endocannabinoid system is deeply involved in sleep regulation, and poor sleep further dysregulates your endocannabinoid system. It's a vicious cycle. What helped me was not trying to force sleep, but creating conditions where sleep could happen. A cool room. A weighted blanket. A consistent wind-down routine that started two hours before bed. No screens. No stressful conversations. Just gentle, boring activities that told my nervous system, "We're done for the day."

Why the wellness industry gets this wrong

I need to be honest with you about something. The wellness industry has co-opted a lot of this language. They talk about "balancing your endocannabinoid system" and sell you expensive supplements, oils, and tinctures that promise to fix everything. Most of them don't work. Some of them might even make things worse.

Here's the truth. Your endocannabinoid system is not a machine that needs to be tuned up. It's a living, adaptive system that responds to your environment, your relationships, and your experiences. The most powerful thing you can do is not buy a product. It's to create conditions where your body can do what it already knows how to do.

That's not sexy. It doesn't sell. But it's real.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He talks about how chronic stress, often rooted in trauma, affects every system in your body, including your endocannabinoid system. It's a hard read. But it's worth it.

I also want to say something about the "just relax" advice. You've probably heard it. "Just do some deep breathing. Just meditate. Just let it go." That advice comes from a place of privilege. It assumes your nervous system is capable of relaxing. For many of us, it's not that simple. Your endocannabinoid system might be so dysregulated that your body literally cannot calm down on command. That's not a moral failing. It's biology.

So here's what I want you to take away from this. Your body has a built-in recovery system. It's called the endocannabinoid system. It's been worn down by stress, trauma, and the demands of modern life. But it's not broken. It's just tired. And you can support it. Not by forcing it. Not by buying things. But by creating small, consistent conditions of safety. Gentle movement. Brief moments of presence. Connection with safe others. Rest that isn't forced.

That's it. That's the work. It's slow. It's boring. And it's the most important thing you can do for your nervous system.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read this. Maybe some tension. Maybe some relief. Maybe just curiosity. That's your endocannabinoid system, doing its best. Thank it. It's been working hard for you, even when you didn't know it existed.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is this classic on healing trauma through the body.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is this look at how trauma affects the brain and body.
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system is this accessible book on polyvagal theory in daily life.
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is this powerful exploration of the mind-body connection.
